project.title: Identification of ubiquitination sites on protein CSDE1 in SHSY5Y cells.
project.projectDescription: This bottom-up proteomics dataset provides a global map of post-translational modifications (PTMs) on the RNA-binding protein CSDE1 in human neuroblastoma SHSY5Y cells. The data was acquired to survey the modification landscape of CSDE1, with a particular observation that lysine (K) residues displayed the most abundant modification signals among all amino acids. This unbiased survey informed subsequent functional studies investigating the role of specific modification sites. The relationship between CSDE1 and the E3 ligase MKRN2 was established in our prior research.
project.sampleProcessingProtocol: This sample was prepared to survey the ubiquitination landscape of the known substrate CSDE1 under conditions where its cognate E3 ligase, MKRN2, was present. CSDE1 was enriched via immunoprecipitation from SHSY5Y cells, followed by standard in-solution digestion (reduction with TCEP, alkylation with NEM, and trypsin cleavage). No further enrichment for ubiquitinated peptides was performed, as the goal was to capture the endogenous modification profile for subsequent targeted validation.
project.dataProcessingProtocol: The raw LC-MS/MS data were processed using Proteome Discoverer. The database search included variable modifications such as Oxidation (M) and GlyGly (K) to identify potential ubiquitination sites. To precisely localize the modification site within each peptide, the Ascore algorithm was applied. Only ubiquitination sites with an Ascore ≥ 6 were retained as reliably localized. From the initial 11 peptide-spectrum matches containing the ubiquitin remnant motif, this filtering step yielded 7 high-confidence ubiquitination sites on CSDE1. These seven sites were subsequently prioritized as candidates for functional validation via site-directed mutagenesis (K-to-R).

Comment[Separation]: Peptides were separated and analyzed by the EASY-nLC system (Thermo Fisher) and the Q Exactive mass spectrometer (Thermo Fisher), respectively.
